title_en: Chapter on Tawassul — Seeking Nearness to Allah
title_ar: باب ما جاء في التوسل بالصالحين
content_en:
Few topics in Islamic theology have generated as much scholarly debate as the question of tawassul — the act of drawing near to Allah through a means or intermediary. This chapter in Kitab al-Tawhid addresses the distinction between the permissible and impermissible forms of tawassul, and it represents one of the most critical practical implications of the book's core thesis.

The chapter examines the verse: 'O you who believe, fear Allah and seek the means (wasilah) of nearness to Him' (Quran 5:35). Scholars unanimously agree that seeking nearness to Allah through one's own righteous deeds, through sincere supplication, through the names and attributes of Allah, and through the living and present intercession of a righteous person (with their knowledge and consent) are all forms of tawassul explicitly endorsed by authentic Prophetic texts.

The story of the three men trapped in a cave, recorded in both al-Bukhari and Muslim, illustrates the permissible form of tawassul through one's own deeds. Each of the three men called upon Allah by mentioning a righteous act they had performed — one who had honored his parents over his own desires, one who had avoided fornication despite having the opportunity, and one who had given an employee his full due and even increased it. In each case, the tawassul was through their own sincerely performed deeds, not through a deceased person or an absent intermediary.

Ibn Abd al-Wahhab's primary concern in this chapter is the practice of supplicating to deceased saints and awliya (friends of Allah) — asking them directly to fulfill one's needs or to intercede with Allah on one's behalf. He argues that this constitutes a form of worship directed at other than Allah, and thus constitutes shirk. His argument is rooted in the understanding that du'a (supplication) is itself a form of worship — a principle established by the hadith 'Supplication is worship' (recorded by Abu Dawud and al-Tirmidhi) — and worship directed to other than Allah is by definition shirk, regardless of the spiritual standing of the person being supplicated.

The author engages with the objection that such practices are merely seeking intercession rather than direct worship. He responds by distinguishing between the Islamic concept of shafa'ah (intercession), which is granted by Allah's permission on the Day of Judgment and cannot be solicited from the deceased in this world, and the polytheistic concept of intercession, in which one approaches a powerful intermediary who has his own independent access to divine favor. The Quran explicitly rejects this latter concept: 'Say: All intercession belongs to Allah entirely' (Quran 39:44).

This chapter must be read within the context of the broader scholarly discussion in which it exists. Many classical scholars, including within the Hanafi and Maliki traditions, have permitted certain forms of tawassul through the Prophet ﷺ or through righteous persons, including after their death. Ibn Abd al-Wahhab's position represents the stricter Hanbali-Athari view, one that draws a sharp line between permissible nearness-seeking and what he characterizes as a slide toward the polytheism that Islam came to eliminate. Understanding this internal scholarly debate is essential for a complete picture of the issue.
